title: St John's Wort, herb profile
subtitle: Hypericum perforatum: the most evidence-supported herbal antidepressant
excerpt: A materia medica profile of St John's Wort, its traditional actions, the clinical evidence for mild-to-moderate depression, drug interactions, and why this is one herb that absolutely requires prescriber consultation before use. Important, well-studied, and significantly interacting.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), St John's Wort entry. Culpeper, The English Physician (1652). Cochrane review: Linde et al. (2008), 'St John's wort for major depression', multiple RCTs showing superiority to placebo and comparable efficacy to standard antidepressants for mild-moderate depression. EMA HMPC monograph on Hyperici herba (2018), full traditional and well-established use status.

glossaryTerms.definition: One of the primary active constituents of St John's Wort, responsible for both its antidepressant mechanism (serotonin reuptake inhibition) and many of its drug interactions via CYP enzyme induction.

attrs.title: Read this before using St John's Wort
attrs.body: St John's Wort has significant drug interactions with many commonly prescribed medications. It induces CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein enzymes, which accelerates the metabolism and reduces the effectiveness of: oral contraceptives, antiretrovirals (HIV medication), warfarin, cyclosporine (transplant medication), digoxin, certain antidepressants, certain antibiotics, and many other drugs. Do not take St John's Wort without first checking for interactions with your prescriber or pharmacist if you take any prescription medication.

content.text: Hypericum perforatum, St John's Wort, is a common perennial wildflower with distinctive small yellow flowers that when held up to light reveal the 'perforations' (translucent oil glands) in the petals. It flowers around the feast of St John (24 June), which explains the name. The fresh or dried flowers and flower buds are the medicinal part. The primary active constituents include
and hypericin. It grows wild across Britain and most of Europe; it is also cultivated for the supplement and herbal medicine trade.

Antidepressant (mild-to-moderate depression), nervine, anxiolytic, wound-healing (oil-based preparations topically), antiviral (traditional use, limited evidence). Culpeper (1652) lists it for 'melancholy' and wound healing. Maud Grieve gives it as a nervine and for 'nervous affections'. The antidepressant mechanism, serotonin, dopamine, and noradrenaline reuptake inhibition, was characterised in the 20th century.

St John's Wort is the most evidence-supported herbal medicine in the western tradition. The Cochrane review by Linde et al. (2008), covering 29 trials with 5,489 participants, found standardised St John's Wort preparations superior to placebo and comparably effective to standard antidepressants for mild-to-moderate depression, with fewer side effects. The EMA has granted it 'well-established use' status (the highest category) rather than merely 'traditional use', it is the only herbal antidepressant to achieve this. This is real clinical evidence, not tradition alone.

Home-prepared St John's Wort infusions from dried herb are significantly less reliable in their hyperforin content than standardised commercial extracts. The clinical trials used standardised preparations (typically 0.3% hypericin, 3, 5% hyperforin). For home use: standardised commercial capsules or tablets are more reliable than home infusions. If making a home tincture or infusion, the dose is not well-characterised, seek herbalist guidance on dose. Onset of action: 4 to 6 weeks for a therapeutic effect. It is not an acute remedy.

St John's Wort induces CYP3A4, CYP2C9, and P-glycoprotein, enzymes and transporters responsible for metabolising and excreting many drugs. This reduces blood levels of: oral contraceptives (risk of contraceptive failure, documented in case reports), warfarin and other anticoagulants, cyclosporine (transplant rejection risk), antiretrovirals, digoxin, theophylline, certain SSRIs (serotonin syndrome risk), certain antibiotics (particularly rifampicin-based), some chemotherapy agents, and others. This is not a theoretical concern, there are documented cases of transplant rejection, contraceptive failure, and HIV viral load rebound linked to St John's Wort use. Always check interactions before starting.

Safe for most adults taking no prescription medication. Photosensitisation at high doses (fair-skinned people should use sun protection while taking it). Do not combine with SSRIs or other antidepressants (serotonin syndrome risk). Not for use in pregnancy or breastfeeding without herbalist advice. Always disclose St John's Wort use to your prescriber and pharmacist. Seek herbalist or GP guidance before starting.
